content.text: Based on the official documentation and industry best practices for long-running agent workflows,

assigns unique identifiers to every agent execution and propagates them across all services, enabling you to trace an agent's complete lifecycle from start to finish—even across async background tasks, checkpoints, and service boundaries
— turning debugging from "what happened?" to "here's exactly where and why it failed."

attrs.alt: Session tracing and distributed run IDs concept diagram

content.text: Record the sequence of steps, tool calls, and decisions in an agent run
Unique identifier that follows a request as it flows through your system across services
Tracing an agent run means you can answer three questions quickly
The sequence of steps, tool calls and decisions
The versioned configuration and policy that drove choices
Safe replay of the run inputs and outcomes
Define identifiers that you can use across logs, traces and dashboards:
Unique per agent execution (can differ from
Monotonic step counter for the run
: These IDs become the backbone for reliability metrics and incident analysis.
A trace ID is a unique identifier that follows a request as it flows through your system

User request → Service A → Service B → Agent → Tool → Database
    ↓
Same trace_id propagates across all services
    ↓
Reconstruct full request lifecycle
: "In distributed systems, trace IDs enable you to correlate operations across multiple services and reconstruct the full request lifecycle".
The Hard Part: Trace Context Propagation
"The hardest part of distributed tracing is not instrumentation — it's trace context propagation across service boundaries"

OpenTelemetry uses W3C Trace Context standard:
    ↓
trace parent header carries traceId and spanId from one service to the next
    ↓
When trace parent header is missing (or not forwarded), each service starts a new trace
    ↓
Waterfall disappears (disconnected fragments)
: "Not caused by missing instrumentation, but by missing propagation".
Represent each step as a span
: Use consistent attribute names so you can aggregate across tools and routes.

# OpenTelemetry: inject/extract pattern
# Service A (producer)
from opentelemetry.trace import set_span_in_context
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.http.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter

# Create trace context
span = tracer.start_span("agent.plan")
context = set_span_in_context(span)

# Inject trace parent into outgoing request
headers = {}
text_encoder.inject(headers, context)

# Service B (consumer)
# Extract trace context from incoming request
context = text_encoder.extract(headers)
span = tracer.start_span("agent.tool", context=context)
: "All spans across the three services share the same
, forming a single trace instead of disconnected fragments".

# Agentuity: standalone code gets same infrastructure
ctx = createAgentContext()

# ctx.run() executes agent with full infrastructure support:
# - Tracing: OpenTelemetry spans with proper hierarchy
# - Sessions: Automatic save/restore via providers
# - Threads: Conversation state management
# - Storage: Full access to kv, stream, vector
# - Background tasks: waitUntil support for fire-and-forget work
# - Session events: Start/complete events for observability

ctx.run(agent, input)
call provides the same infrastructure as HTTP request handlers".

# Proactive messaging (async work)
# Extracted context is passed through context.with() so spans still join the right trace

async def background_task(ctx, input):
    # Continue with inherited trace context
    await ctx.run(agent, input)
: "Without trace continuity, these workflows become difficult to debug and reason about".
Map to specific versions and routes
: "Tracing is not only for incidents. It also drives improvement".

Agent Sessions persist conversation and event history
    ↓
Pin to custom session ID that maps to your own CRM or DB record
    ↓
Track agent lifecycle across multiple invocations
: "You can pin them to a custom session ID that maps to your own CRM or DB record, so the agent's context persists across multiple interactions".
Attach Version Metadata to Every Run
Most incidents are caused by change. Make change visible by recording version metadata on each run
Which policy module or ruleset applied
: "This is what turns debugging into 'compare before and after' instead of guesswork".
Capture Minimum Data Needed for Safe Replay
Replay is powerful, but it can also create privacy and security risk. Store only what you need
Traces tell you what happened. Decision logs tell you why it was allowed

For agentic systems, you often need both:
    ↓
Record approvals and policy blocks
    ↓
Record key routing decisions and fallbacks
    ↓
Keep audit trails for sensitive tool actions
: Create audit trails for compliance and incident investigation.
Think of session tracing and distributed run IDs like

: when you book a flight (agent request), you get a unique tracking number (run ID). Every time you check-in, board, or fly (service A → service B → agent → tool), the same tracking number is recorded. If something goes wrong (flight delayed), you can look up the tracking number and see the entire journey: where it departed, where it stopped, where it was delayed. Without the tracking number, you'd have to guess which flight you were talking about.
Tracing = answer what happened, why it happened, can we reproduce it
Run ID model: request_id (per request), run_id (per execution), step_id (monotonic counter), tool_call_id (per tool)
Trace ID: unique identifier that follows request across services, correlates operations
Hardest part: trace context propagation across service boundaries (not instrumentation)
Propagation: W3C Trace Context standard - trace parent header carries traceId and spanId
Missing propagation = disconnected fragments (most tracing issues)
Span taxonomy: agent.plan, agent.retrieve, agent.model, agent.tool, agent.validate
Async work: use context.with() so spans still join the right trace
Standalone execution: ctx.run() provides same infrastructure as HTTP (tracing, sessions, storage)
Version metadata: prompt_version, policy_version, tool_contract_version, route_config_version
Replay: capture minimum data (redacted input, masked tool args, retrieval snippets, model params)
Traces tell what happened, decision logs tell why it was allowed
Benefits: find slow steps, repetitive calls, validation failures, correlate complaints
"Tracing agent runs with run IDs, spans for tools and retrieval, version metadata, and safe replay for debugging incidents".
When working with session tracing and distributed run ids, following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with session tracing and distributed run ids. Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of session tracing and distributed run ids scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
